title: Aliases and Shortcuts
intro: You type the same long commands dozens of times a day -- git status, npm run dev, ssh into your server. What if you could replace any command with a short nickname? Aliases let you create custom shortcuts so a 40-character command becomes 2 letters.

teach.title: Aliases = Speed Dial Buttons
teach.body: Remember speed dial on old phones? Instead of dialing a full 10-digit number every time, you press one button and it dials the whole number for you. **Aliases work exactly the same way.** Instead of typing `git status` every time, you create an alias `gs` that expands into the full command. One shortcut, same result -- but way faster.

teach.title: Aliases = Nicknames
teach.body: Your friend's name is Alexander, but everyone calls him Alex. Shorter to say, same person. **Aliases are nicknames for commands.** `ll` is a nickname for `ls -la`, `gp` is a nickname for `git push`. The command doesn't change -- you just give it a shorter name that's easier to type and remember.

teach.title: How Aliases Work
teach.body:
An alias is a custom shortcut you define in your terminal:

- **`alias shortname='full command'`** -- Creates a temporary alias for this session
- **Add to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`** -- Makes the alias permanent (loads every time you open a terminal)
- **`unalias shortname`** -- Removes an alias
- **`alias`** -- Lists all current aliases

Aliases are stored in your shell config file. When you open a new terminal, it reads that file and loads all your aliases automatically.

teach.title: Popular Aliases Developers Use
teach.code:
# Git shortcuts
alias gs='git status'
alias ga='git add .'
alias gc='git commit -m'
alias gp='git push'

# Navigation shortcuts
alias ll='ls -la'
alias ..='cd ..'
alias ...='cd ../..'

# Project shortcuts
alias dev='npm run dev'
alias build='npm run build'
teach.annotation: These aliases save you thousands of keystrokes over a project. 'gs' instead of 'git status' saves 8 characters -- multiply that by 50 times a day and you're saving serious time.

teach.body: Professional developers who vibecode for a living have 20+ aliases configured. It's not about being lazy -- it's about removing friction so you can ship faster. When you're billing clients hourly, every second saved with aliases is money earned. A developer with a polished terminal setup looks and works like a pro.

Aliases are custom shortcuts for long commands. Define them with `alias name='command'` and make them permanent by adding them to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc file. Start with git and npm aliases -- they'll save you the most time.

steps.instruction: Create a temporary alias called 'gs' that runs 'git status'. This will only last for the current session.
steps.hint: Use the alias command: alias shortname='full command'. The short name is gs and the full command is git status.

steps.instruction: Test your new alias by typing 'gs' to run git status.
steps.output:
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
steps.hint: Just type your alias name 'gs' and press enter -- it will expand to 'git status' automatically.

steps.instruction: Now let's make a permanent alias. Add the line alias dev='npm run dev' to the end of your ~/.zshrc file using echo and append redirect.
steps.hint: Use echo with >> to append a line to a file: echo "alias dev='npm run dev'" >> ~/.zshrc

steps.instruction: Reload your shell config so the new alias takes effect without opening a new terminal.
steps.hint: The 'source' command re-reads a file. Run 'source ~/.zshrc' to reload your config.

exercise.completionMessage: You've created both a temporary alias and a permanent one! From now on, 'gs' runs git status and 'dev' starts your development server. Add more aliases to your ~/.zshrc as you discover commands you type often -- your future self will thank you.
